content:
12 min read
Just now

--

If listeners cannot reliably hear the difference, then the fight over AI music is not really about music. It is about the story we need a song to carry before we let ourselves care.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Image created with AI assistance by J.S. MatkowskiSomeone recently made a claim I keep hearing more often: most people do not want to listen to AI music.
Not because they are confused. Not because they are afraid. Not because they have failed to understand the tool lineage from synthesizers to samplers to Auto-Tune to digital workstations. They simply do not want it. They do not want it in their playlists. They do not want it in their recommendations. They do not want it competing with human artists. They do not want to discover afterward that the song they gave emotional attention to was generated by a machine-assisted process rather than made by a person in the older, more familiar sense.
That claim deserves to be taken seriously.
A lot of people inside the AI music community do not want to take it seriously because it is harder to answer than the usual accusation. If someone says AI music is fake, lazy, or artistically illegitimate, creators can argue back. They can point to vision, curation, iteration…
